era_context: Early Dynastic Period I in Mesopotamia; First Dynasty of Egypt in its early phase. The world's first writing systems are emerging or recently established. Urban civilization is consolidating in the Nile Valley and Mesopotamia. Most of the world remains non-literate and archaeologically sparse for this specific year.
geographic_coverage_gaps: South Asia (Indus Valley Civilization is emerging but undocumented for this specific year)

events.title: Early Dynastic I Mesopotamia — temple economy and cuneiform administration
events.description: During the Early Dynastic I period, Mesopotamian city-states are organizing economic life around temple complexes. Cuneiform writing, recently invented, is used to record temple inventories, labor allocations, and commodity distributions. This represents one of the earliest uses of writing for administrative purposes. The temple serves as the primary institution for resource management, religious authority, and political control. Economic activity is largely redistributive, centered on the temple rather than market exchange.

sources.name: Early cuneiform tablets from Uruk and other sites (administrative records)
Archaeological evidence from temple complexes at Uruk, Lagash, and Ur

events.certainty_note: The Early Dynastic I period is archaeologically attested; this year is assigned by convention within the broader period (c. 3100–2900 BCE). Specific events for this individual year are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for this year. The Early Dynastic I period is known from archaeological context and administrative tablets, but these do not provide year-by-year narrative history. Assigning events to individual years in this period is largely conventional.
historiographic_note: This year is at the very edge of written history. No contemporary narrative sources exist for this specific year. All knowledge comes from archaeological context (stratigraphy, artifact typology) and administrative cuneiform tablets that do not bear dates in the modern sense. The chronology for this period is uncertain by ±50–100 years depending on the system used. The historical record is dominated by Mesopotamian and Egyptian sources; the rest of the world is entirely absent from written documentation.
